Tafseer:
1. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ha, Meem. A revelation from the Most Merciful, the Merciful. (1-2)

All 'Ha-Meem' Surahs Share the Same Character and Temperament

'Ha, Meem' is the Qur'anic name of this Surah. We have pointed out in the preceding Surah that all Surahs designated by this name share common themes. Although the construction of the discourse can also be such that it is considered the subject (mubtada) and the subsequent sentence as its predicate (khabar), in our view, it is an independent sentence, and the subject is omitted here. We have opened it in the translation.

In 'A revelation from the Most Merciful, the Merciful' (Tanzeelum-minar-Rahmanir-Raheem) as well, the subject is, in my view, omitted—meaning this Qur'an is sent down by the Most Merciful God. It contains within itself both consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) and a threat and warning for the deniers of the Qur'an. The aspect of consolation for the Prophet (pbuh) is: 'You have neither composed this Book yourself, nor have you requested it from God of your own accord; rather, your Lord Himself has sent it down and is sending it down upon you with highly elaborate care, step by step, so rest assured that He Himself will manifest its truths and He Himself will pave the way for it.' The God Who has sent down this Book is no helpless being, but 'Al-Aziz'—meaning the Dominant and Powerful. He can do whatever He wills, and no one can obstruct His will. But along with being Exalted in Might, He is also Wise ('Al-Hakeem'); for this reason, if some obstructions are faced in the path now or will be faced in the future, attribute them to the wisdom of God. Nothing happens without the command of God, and His every action is based on wisdom and purpose.

The aspect of warning for its deniers is: Allah Almighty is sending down this Book with highly elaborate care and graduation for the guidance of people. Its right is that people value this blessing and obtain guidance and light from it. If, contrary to this, they adopt the path of denial, they must remember that this is not the request of a petitioner, but the revealed scripture of guidance from the Exalted in Might, the Wise God; therefore, both its acceptance and the consequences of its opposition are highly significant and far-reaching.

The Specific Right of the Arabs over the Qur'an

This is a subsequent predicate (khabar) after the first, and is a detail of that mercy and favor of Allah which He showed specifically to the Arabs by sending down the Qur'an in the Arabic language so that they would face no difficulty in understanding this discourse. If this had not been so, they would have raised the objection: why did Allah Almighty not clarify His revelation to them in their own language? Thus, further below in this very Surah, the explanation of this has been given as follows.

"And if We had made it a foreign Qur'an, they would have said, 'Why are its verses not explained in detail?' Is it a foreign [tongue] and an Arab [messenger]?"

In 'for a people who know' (li-qawmin ya'lamoon), the verb 'know' is, in our view, used in the sense of intending to know. That is: We made this entire arrangement for those who intend to know and understand. As for those who are devoid of the desire to know, all efforts are useless for them. Within this style of expression, there is also encouragement and incitement for the Arabs—showing that they should be desirous of knowing and understanding, because they have been unlettered, and now for the first time, Allah Almighty has sent down His Book in their own language out of His mercy for their education.

The Far-Reaching Effects of Both the Acceptance and Denial of the Qur'an

'as a giver of glad tidings and a warner' (basheeran wa nadheera)—this describes the second attribute of the Qur'an, that it has been sent down as a giver of glad tidings and a warner. For those who accept it, it is glad tidings of success in both this world and the Hereafter, and for those who deny it, it is the precursor of God's punishment. Meaning: let no one consider it an easy thing. It is now the greatest mercy and also the greatest punishment; therefore, those who are dedicated to opposing it should think through the consequences of this opposition.

The Qur'an is Not Mere Advice, but a Divine Warning

'but most of them turn away, so they do not hear'—meaning the majority of people did not value this importance, but considered it an ordinary thing and turned away from it, being not ready in any way to listen and understand. They do not realize that this is not merely the advice of some preacher, but a divine warning, and every single thing of which it is giving news is bound to happen.

This describes their turning away and not listening—showing that they say with great arrogance: 'Our hearts are entirely closed to that to which you invite us'—meaning this invitation to Tawhid and the Hereafter which you have brought does not affect our hearts in any way. This is exactly what is quoted from the tongues of the Jews with the words: 'Our hearts are wrapped.' The analysis of the word 'akinnah' (covers) has passed in its proper place.

After 'and in our ears is deafness' (wa fee adhanina waqr), the words 'from hearing the Qur'an' or a similar expression is omitted. The contrast with 'to that to which you invite us' (mimma tad'oona ilayh) points to this omission, which we have opened in the translation—meaning: 'Our ears are deaf to hearing this Qur'an which you are reciting to us.'

'and between us and you is a partition'—meaning: 'A wall of difference regarding beliefs, ideas, ways, and religion has been set up between us and you, such that there remains no possibility of our ever meeting.'

The Final Demand of the Opponents of the Qur'an

'so work; indeed, we are working'—meaning: 'When such a wall has been set up between us that we can never meet, then do whatever you please, and we will also do whatever we are going to do.' Although it is not apparent from the words, keeping in view the words 'giver of glad tidings and a warner' which passed above, within 'so work' (fa'mal) is implied their demand for the punishment—implying: 'Bring that punishment of which you are warning us daily in your Qur'an if you are truthful; otherwise, we are going to do whatever we are going to do.'

He has no partner or associate; so turn to Him with complete devotion, ask forgiveness for the polytheism you have been involved in until now, and return to your true Lord. And hear also: for these polytheists is destruction—who do not spend in the way of Allah, but sit carelessly relying on their assumed intercessors. It states that they are the true deniers of the Hereafter.

The Word 'Zakah' in the General Sense of Spending

In 'those who do not give zakah' (alladhina la yu'toona-z-zakah), 'zakah' refers to spending in the way of Allah (infaq fee sabeelillah). This word was used in this general sense during the Meccan period of Islam as well. In the Medinan period, it took a structured form, and then began to be used specifically for it. Here, the word is used in its general sense, and was well-known in this sense at that time.

The objective of this reply is: 'Bringing the punishment of which the Qur'an is warning is not my job, but is the job of Allah; however, listen to this warning well: for the polytheists who rely on their partners and intercessors instead of faith and righteous deeds, is destruction.'

Believing in the Hereafter alongside Polytheism and Intercession falls under the Category of Its Denial

'and they are, in the Hereafter, disbelievers' (wa hum bil-akhirati hum kafaroon). In this clause, the repetition of the subject ('hum') produces the meaning of restrictiveness. Its correct meaning is: they are the true deniers of the Hereafter. The benefit of this restrictiveness is that let no one remain in the misconception: since they believe in the Hereafter—though with the concept of shirk and intercession—some credibility must belong to this belief of theirs. It states: 'Never! Those who believe that their partners and intercessors will save them regardless of their deeds do not believe in the Hereafter, but are its primary deniers.' The reason for saying this with such force and emphasis is that they negated that justice and wisdom of God upon which the Hereafter is based. Other deniers merely consider it improbable or are in doubt, but they have demolished the entire philosophy of the Resurrection. Below you will see that the Qur'an has clearly described shirk as disbelief (kufr) as well. The reason is that shirk negates all basic attributes of Allah Almighty, after which believing or not believing in Him becomes identical.

Glad Tidings for Those Who Remain Firm on Pure Tawhid

This is the glad tidings in contrast to the warning—showing that with Allah, an uninterrupted reward is only for those who adopt the path of faith and righteous deeds—meaning they believe in Tawhid without any trace of shirk, and perform those duties of worship and obedience which are obligatory upon them with complete sincerity and steadfastness. Some have interpreted 'ghayru mamnoon' (uninterrupted) differently, but the parallels in the Qur'an support the interpretation we have adopted. In some places, this same theme is expressed with the words: 'a gift uninterrupted' (ata'an ghayra majdhoodh). Obviously, this refers to a favor whose flow is never cut off.

Proofs of Tawhid in the System of the Universe

Now, attention is drawn to those signs of the Creator's power, wisdom, lordship, and His Tawhid which are prominent in the system of the heavens and the earth, and below in verse 12, the summary of this discussion is concluded: 'That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing' (dhalika taqdeerul-Azizil-Aleem)—meaning whoever reflects on these aspects of this universe is forced to admit that it neither came into existence by accident, nor is it a plaything of a player, nor is it a battlefield of different deities; but it has come into existence by the planning, knowledge, and wisdom of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing God. This theme is spread over four verses; for this reason, to facilitate readers, we will first discuss the verses individually, and then present the summary of the discussion to clear any doubts that arise.

Let us take the first verse of this series, quoted above. It states: 'Are you indeed disbelieving in Him Who created the earth in two days, and associating partners with Him? That is the Lord of the worlds!'

Believing in God is Credible Only When It is with the Acknowledgment of All His Attributes

Here, the first noteworthy point is: although the address is to the polytheists of the Quraysh who did not deny God but associated partners with Him, the Qur'an has described their shirk as disbelief ('a'innakum latakfuroona'). The reason is that believing in God is credible only when it is with all His attributes and all their requirements. If a person believes in God but in such a way that His attributes are negated, then this belief is not credible in religion; rather, it is in reality disbelief. For this reason, the Qur'an has repeatedly described shirk as disbelief and addressed the polytheists with the words: 'O disbelievers!' (Ya ayyuha al-Kafiroon). Keep in view also what we wrote above regarding the polytheists' being deniers of the Hereafter. Both of these points are based on the same principle.

Divine Days are Different from Our Days

In 'in two days' (fee yawmayn), 'days' does not refer to our literal days, but, as we have explained in its proper place, refers to divine days which are equal to a thousand years, or in some cases, fifty thousand years, in our count; therefore, they should be taken in the sense of periods or stages.

Details of the Creation of the Heavens and the Earth

In other places in the Qur'an, the creation of the heavens and the earth and their attachments is described as occurring in six days. Here, the details of this total number of days are described—showing how many days were spent on the creation of what. It states: 'Allah created the earth in two days. In this work of His, He had no partner, associate, or helper, but you associate partners with Him! Whereas He Who is its Creator is also the Lord of this earth and of the entire universe.' The meaning is: He alone is the Lord and He alone deserves to be so because He is the Creator; but your intellect is so turned upside down that contrary to reason and nature and without any proof, you have set up partners and rivals for Him! It should be kept in mind here that the Arab polytheists accepted Allah Almighty as the Creator of the heavens and the earth and of everything, but despite this, they associated partners with Him; here, proof is established upon this accepted belief of theirs, and the tone is of reproach.

The Earth and Some of Its Signs

This is a detail of the blessings of the earth—stating that Allah alone set up mountains in it. One wisdom of setting up mountains is described in another place—that they maintain the balance of the earth; otherwise, there was a danger that it would tilt along with all its creations. The words 'from above it' (min fawqiha) are meant to draw attention to them—showing that these signs of Allah Almighty's power are not hidden, but are present on the earth where everyone can see them. In Surah Al-Ghashiyah, attention is drawn to the mountains from this very aspect:

'Then do they not look at the camels - how they are created? And at the sky - how it is raised? And at the mountains - how they are erected? And at the earth - how it is spread?' (Al-Ghashiyah: 17–20).

'and He blessed it and determined therein its sustenance'—meaning He placed blessings in it and determined all kinds of provisions in it.

The Earth and Its Blessings

It is of the miracle of this blessing that it grows all kinds of vegetation whose fruits and flowers are of use to man and other creations; it is of its benefit that a person sows a single seed, and the earth returns its harvest to him in the form of hundreds of seeds. A person plants a seed or a cutting in the earth, and for a long period, he and his successors eat its fruit. Moreover, it is of the fruit of this blessing that as man turns over its layers through science, treasures upon treasures come out of it, and it is clearly seen that man's science will be exhausted but the treasures of the earth are not going to end.

'and determined therein its sustenance'—meaning He Who created this family of Adam on this earth also determined its provision in it in accordance with the number of creations He has created or will create in it. These provisions will continuously be extracted by man through his planning until the Day of Judgment. The provision of every creation that will be born on this earth has been entrusted to the earth by Allah, and the plan to extract it has also been inspired to man.

The Total Number of Days Spent on the Creation of the Heavens and the Earth

'in four days' (fee arba'ati ayyam)—all these works were completed in four days. That is, the days of the creation of the earth and the days of setting up mountains in it and determining sustenance, all combined make four days. It has summed up all the days spent on these works.

Allah Has Arranged Food for Every Kind of Creation in Accordance with Their Instinctive Demands

'equally for those who ask' (sawa'an lis-sa'ileen)—meaning Allah Almighty has arranged these provisions in accordance with the instinctive needs of all those creations He has created and for whose survival He has placed needs within them. It is not that some creations have come into existence but the food required for their survival has not come into existence. On the peaks of the mountains, in the depths of the earth, in the darknesses of the oceans, wherever any small or large creation exists, its natural provision is present in its surroundings. A goat lives by eating grass; for it, Allah has created grass. A lion lives by eating meat; Allah has given it weapons of prey and also created animals for its prey. And it is also clearly seen that no one had to forcefully adapt themselves to their needs, but whatever anyone received, they received in accordance with their instinctive demands. Attention is drawn to this reality: this can be the glory of Allah alone—that He creates so many kinds of creations and then provides food for them in accordance with their instinctive demands. Who besides God can be competent to do this?

The Word 'Su'al' in a Specific Meaning

The word 'su'al' (asking) here is in the same meaning as in verse 34 of Surah Ibrahim: 'And He gave you from all you asked of Him'—meaning He provided for you those things whose need He placed within you.

A Baseless Argument of the Socialists

There is no need to refute the attempt of the socialists to find proof of food equality from this clause. We have explained the correct interpretation of the verse, and this is our responsibility. Creating the capability to understand the correct thing and accept it is in the power of no one but Allah Almighty.

Was the Heaven Created First or the Earth?

'Istawa ila as-shai' means he turned toward it or he intended it. It states that after these stages of the creation of the earth, He turned toward the heaven while it was smoke. By 'smoke' (dukhan), dust is meant, or in the astronomers' terminology, let us call it nebula. This shows that at that time, the heaven was present in an initial and incomplete form. This form of its, obviously, must have come into existence during those two days in which the initial creation of the earth was completed; for this reason, by 'turning toward it' (istawa), that attention is meant which Allah Almighty paid to give the heaven its final completed form. Thus, from the subsequent verses, it is clear that at this stage, Allah Almighty formed the heaven into seven heavens and made every heaven bound to its duty in the system of the universe. This shows that the creation of both the heavens and the earth was initiated simultaneously, but just as in a vast construction, under the requirements of construction materials, sometimes work is done on one of its corners and sometimes on another, similarly, the work of the construction of the heavens and the earth was done; for this reason, this question is unnecessary: was the earth created first or the heaven? The planning of a house is necessarily done at the same time. The materials required for the construction of its every part are also arranged. Although the work begins with foundations and walls, before their completion, the work on the roof begins. While some works regarding the roof are still remaining, the plastering of walls begins. Once this is finished, the completion of the roof demands attention. Following this, attention is paid to the completion of the floor. In short, the construction of a house is a composite and collective matter, and it must be reflected upon in this very capacity.

Although this ordinary example has no comparison with the heavens and the earth, to understand the matter, the heavens and the earth must also be compared to it, because the Qur'an has mentioned both of them as a house. Sometimes the mention of the heaven is made first, and sometimes of the earth. Sometimes the thought arises that the roof was made first, and sometimes the doubt arises that the floor was completed before the roof; whereas such doubts arise purely because both the heavens and the earth are not looked at as a single composite whole, but individually. We limit our discussion to these allusions here. God willing, further explanation will follow under verses 30–33 of Surah An-Nazi'at.

Every Thing in This Universe is Muslim by Virtue of Its Instinct

'and said to it and to the earth, "Come [into being], willingly or unwillingly." They said, "We come, willingly."': That is, Allah Almighty, after bringing the heavens and the earth into existence, instructed: 'Both of you must necessarily obey Me, whether you wish or do not wish.' They replied: 'We are present for obedient compliance.' This shows that the compliance of those things which comply with Allah Almighty instinctively is also not forced, but willing, because Allah Almighty has made their instinct in the mold of Islam. As if everything in this universe is Muslim by virtue of its instinct. If the angels do not disobey Allah, the cause is not that they are forced to obey Him, but Allah has colored their nature with the color of Islam in such a way that no violation ever occurs on their part.

'Come [into being], willingly' (A'tiya taw'an) is of the same style as Hazrat Sulaiman (peace be upon him) had written to the Queen of Saba in his letter: 'And come to me in submission (Muslimin).' Those who have interpreted its meaning differently have done so due to unfamiliarity with the style of the language.

Inanimate Things are Also Intelligent in Understanding and Complying with their Lord's Commands

From this verse, it is also learned that those things in this universe which we place in the category of inanimate things are also intelligent in understanding their Lord's commands, replying to His statements, complying with His instructions, and exalting and praising Him. Thus, in the statement quoted from the tongues of the heavens and the earth here, the word 'ta'i'een' (willingly) has occurred, which is suitable only for intelligent and willing entities. If we do not understand their glorification or cannot address them, this does not give us the right to consider them entirely devoid of intellect, but this is merely the limitation of our knowledge. Allah Almighty understands the glorification and praise of every single atom of this universe, and every atom complies with His commands after understanding them.

The Completion of the Seven Heavens

This shows that at the time He turned toward the heaven, the raw material (hayula) of the seven heavens was present, though it still stood in need of the finishing touch. This is pointed to by the plural pronoun and the subsequent contexts. It states: He decided their affairs—that they should become seven heavens.

'and He revealed in each heaven its affair'—meaning He revealed to every heaven its specific duty in the collective system of this universe.

'And We adorned the nearest heaven with lamps'—and We adorned the lowest sky with the lamps of the stars. The change in the style of the discourse should be kept in view—that the preceding statements are in the third-person style, and this statement is in the first-person style. The eloquence of this change has been pointed to in its proper place.

'and as protection' (wa hifdhan)—this verbal noun (masdar) is for the emphasis of an omitted verb—meaning We protected the lowest sky thoroughly from the intrusion of the devils from among the Jinn. Its explanation has passed in various places, and further details will, God willing, follow in the commentary of Surah Al-Jinn. All these works were completed in two days, and collectively, this made six days. This is the detail of that summary which has passed in other places in the Qur'an regarding the creation of the heavens and the earth—showing that Allah Almighty created them in six days.

The Summary of the Discussion

'That is the determination of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing'—this places the summary of the discussion before us: whoever reflects on this workshop of the universe will proclaim that this is the miracle of the planning of the Exalted in Might, the Knowing God. That is, this is not a thing that came into existence by accident, but contains a highly wise planning, and this planning belongs to a Being Who is dominant over everything. Nothing is outside His sphere of power. At the same time, His knowledge is all-encompassing—meaning He is aware of every single thing of the distant corners of this universe, is aware of its needs, and knows its specific use in the collective system. Keep this summary in view, and connect the initial question: 'Are you indeed disbelieving in Him...' with it; then the statement is as if: 'this universe, by its very existence, is testifying to the fact that it has been made by the Exalted in Might, the Knowing God and is in His تصرف alone, but your ignorance is such that you are denying Him by making several assumed deities His partners!' This is asked in the form of a question to express wonder and aversion. The meaning is: there is no cause of justification for this kind of foolishness, but when the intellect is turned upside down, no foolishness remains far from man.

The Teaching of These Verses

Although we have been pointing to the teaching of these verses while explaining them, since it relates to the basic wisdom of religion, we remind of it again at the end.

* First, this world has come into existence step by step in accordance with a determined project, with highly elaborate care. The idea is entirely false that someone has made it merely as a plaything and that it will continue as it is or end as it is. This care is an undeniable proof of its being purposeful, and its being purposeful logically requires the Hereafter.

* Second, its Creator possesses infinite power and unlimited knowledge; for this reason, He neither stood in need of anyone's help in this work, nor was anyone capable of helping Him.

* Third, the heavens and the earth have combined to form a home in which Allah Almighty has settled man; therefore, the idea is self-evidently false that someone else has control over its roof while someone else is the owner of its floor; rather, the compatibility of both the heavens and the earth is proof that the Exalted in Might, the Knowing God Who created them is also the One Who controls them.

* Fourth, the all-encompassing system of lordship in this world is proof that He is the Creator; therefore, the servants must turn to Him alone to ask, because He alone is the Giver of benefit and harm.

* Fifth, this vast system of lordship requires that a Day comes in which people present themselves before their Benefactor—to be questioned regarding His blessings. Those who recognized their right will receive its reward, and those who showed ungratefulness will face its punishment.

2. Subsequent Theme: Verses 13–24

Next, to warn the Quraysh, the mention of some historical examples of that disgraceful end has occurred which the deniers of the messengers must face. The sequence of the discourse is entirely clear. Recite the verses.

3. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

The mention of the Quraysh's turning away has passed in verses 4–5 above. Pointing to this, He stated: if they have decided only to turn away, then warn them: 'I warn you of a thunderbolt of punishment like the punishment that came upon 'Aad, Thamud, and other nations of the same conduct.' From the style of the expression, it is clear that the mention of 'Aad and Thamud is by way of example. The objective is to point to the end of all those nations who were destroyed for the crime of denying the messengers, and whose mention has passed in detail in the previous Surahs.

Mention of 'Sayhiqa' (Thunderbolt) as a Prominent Sign of the Punishment

'Sayhiqa' (thunderbolt) is mentioned as a prominent sign of that punishment which destroyed these nations. We have explained in detail in other places that the punishment which came upon these nations was a combination of severe wind from the north, hail, and terrifying thunder and lightning. Thus, in the Qur'an, it is expressed with different words. Here, its prominent sign 'Sayhiqa' is used, but the objective is to point to all those attachments which have occurred in other places and some of which are also coming below.

The Reason for Bringing the Word 'Messengers' in the Plural

Although only two messengers—Hazrat Hud and Hazrat Salih (peace be upon them)—are mentioned as being sent to these nations, the word 'messengers' (rusul) is used here in the plural for the reason to which we pointed above—that the mention of these nations is by way of example. The objective is to point to all those nations who followed their wrong way and denied their messengers.

Expressing the All-Encompassing Efforts of the Messengers

'before them and behind them, [saying], "Worship not except Allah"' (min bayni aydeehim wa min khalfihim). This describes those untiring, all-encompassing, and day-and-night efforts which those messengers dedicated to deliver the invitation of Tawhid to their nations. Iblis had challenged: 'I will surely come to them from before them and from behind them...' (Al-A'raf: 17) to mislead the progeny of Adam. The Qur'an, by describing this attribute of the messengers, as it were, clarified that Allah Almighty also sent such messengers to save these nations from the trials of Iblis who did their best to make them understand from every direction.

The Nations' Reply to Their Messengers

'They said, "If Our Lord..."'—meaning they valued the efforts of their messengers by denying their messengership—claiming that if Allah Almighty had to send a messenger, He would send the angels, not humans like themselves; and since you are humans like us, we are not ready to accept your messengership. Therefore, we clearly refuse to accept that message (pointing to the message of Tawhid) with which you have been sent. The sarcasm in 'we are in that with which you were sent, disbelievers' (Inna bima ursiltum bihi kafiuroon) is not hidden from those of taste. Their meaning was: 'We clearly deny that message of which you claim to be the carrier—meaning we neither accept you as messengers nor your message as a message. For this reason, do not try to put any such pressure on us.'

The Attitude of 'Aad

This explains their attitude which they adopted against their messengers' warnings. First, in terms of historical priority, 'Aad is taken. It states that upon receiving the power and glory which Allah Almighty bestowed upon 'Aad, instead of being grateful to Him, they became arrogant in the earth. Their 'arrogance' (istakbaroo) is described with the words 'without right' (bighayril-haqq). The reason, as we have explained, is that pride is suitable only for Him Who is the Creator and Owner of this entire universe; it is not permissible for anyone else.

'And they said, "Who is greater than us in strength?"'—this is the explanation of their arrogance: when Allah's messenger warned them that if they remained firm on this rebellion of theirs, they would soon be seized in God's grasp, they replied: 'If our way had been of destruction, would its result be that we have no equal in power and glory today! For this reason, our view is that we are not set out on the path of destruction. Rather, those who warn us of the punishment are crazy.'

'Did they not see...': This is the reply to their aforementioned foolish challenge—stating: if they had utilized their intellect, they would have easily understood that the God Who created them and this entire universe is far greater than them in strength. He can seize them whenever and from wherever He wills, and no one can obstruct His will.

'And they were Our signs denying' relates to the actual sequence of the discourse. The clause 'Did they not see...' has occurred in the middle as a swift reply to their challenge. The meaning is: they did not want to hear or understand anything due to their arrogance. Whatever signs Allah's messenger recited to them, they denied them with complete audacity.

The Punishment upon 'Aad

'Reeh sarsar' refers to that severe wind which blows from the north during the severe winter in Arabia, and which is accompanied by clouds of winter and thunder and lightning.

'Ayyam nahisat' (unlucky days/days of misfortune) is used for those days of severe winter in which due to the intensity of the cold, a kind of sadness, depression, and dryness covers everything.

It states: since they adopted arrogance and turned away from the truth, We sent upon them a furious wind during the days of winter, to make them taste the punishment of disgrace in the worldly life—which destroyed them completely. In this way, they were disgraced before those to whom they said: 'Who is greater than us in strength?', and also before other nations. And after this punishment of the world, they must face the punishment of the Hereafter, which will be even more disgraceful, because its disgrace will be eternal and in front of the entire creation.

'and they will not be helped'—and on that Day, no help will come to them from any side. Neither will their power and numbers, of which they were proud, be of any use to them, nor will those deities protect them in whose defense they denied Allah and His messengers.

The Attitude of Thamud and Their Punishment

It states: the same was the case with Thamud after 'Aad. We opened the way of guidance for them as well, but they preferred their deviation over Our guidance; ultimately, as a retribution for their deeds, We seized them in a disgraceful punishment, and they were completely destroyed. It should be kept in mind that the nature of their punishment was also almost the same as has been explained under 'Aad's punishment. In the light of the parallels in the Qur'an, we have presented its details in its proper place.

"And We saved those who believed and used to fear Allah." (18)

This points to those whom Allah Almighty saved from these punishments. The Sunnah of Allah is: those who believed in the messengers and, being warned, adopted a life of piety, Allah Almighty saved them from the punishment. In this verse, the reference is to the believers of both nations, 'Aad and Thamud. In other places in the Qur'an, it is clarified that those who believed, Allah saved them along with the messenger.

"And [warn of] the Day when the enemies of Allah will be gathered to the Fire and they will be [driven] in rows," (19)

The Disgrace of the Hereafter after the Punishment of the World

Following the punishment of the world, this mentions that disgrace and punishment which they will face in the Hereafter.

After 'yuhsharu' (will be gathered), the preposition 'ila' is an indication that this word here encompasses the meaning of 'yusaqoona' (will be driven). 'Yooza'oon' (driven in rows) has been explained under verse 17 of Surah An-Naml. In our view, its correct meaning is: they will be divided into ranks and classes.

It states: warn of that Day when these enemies of Allah will be driven and gathered at the gates of Hell, and they will be divided into classes. Those who deserve a certain depth of Hell will enter through their specific gate. This shows that different criminals will be dealt with differently, in accordance with their deeds.

It states that when all are gathered at the gates of Hell, Allah Almighty will face no difficulty in knowing who belongs to what rank of criminals and deserves what depth of Hell, because on that Day, no one will be able to hide any of their deeds. A person's ears, eyes, and even his skin will testify to all his deeds.

'Julood' (skins) is mentioned after 'ears' and 'eyes' as a general term following specific terms. The specific testimony of hearing and sight is obvious, because these are the recorders of everything; but there are other organs and limbs with which a person performs evil or good. The word 'julood' has encompassed all of them. It is as if on that Day, every hair of a person's body will become a tongue to testify. It should be kept in mind that 'julood' has occurred in the Qur'an in the sense of the body's hair. We have explained this under the verse: 'whereat shiver the skins of those...' (Az-Zumar: 23).

The Organs' Testimony against Oneself

Hearing the testimony of their own organs, they will beat their heads and reproach them: 'Why did you testify against us?' They will reply: 'Today everything has been made to speak, so He Who has made everything speak today has also made us speak.' Parallels in the Qur'an show that on the Day of Judgment, everything will relate its entire story. For example, He says: 'When the earth is shaken with its [final] earthquake, And the earth discharges its burdens, And man says, "What is [wrong] with it?" - That Day, it will report its news, Because your Lord has commanded [i.e., inspired] it.' (Al-Zalzalah: 1–4).

It should be kept in mind that everything in the heavens and the earth is speaking even today, because everything is a sign of Allah, testifying to His power, wisdom, mercy, and lordship to establish proof against man; but today, their testimony is heard only by those whose hearts are awake. However, on the Day of Judgment, everyone will hear their testimony and everyone will confirm it, because the veils that are present today will be removed on that Day.

'And He created you the first time, and to Him you will be returned.' Although this statement can also be a part of what was said by the 'julood' (skins), in my view, it is conjoined with the actual sequence of the discourse. The mention of questioning the 'julood' and their reply has occurred in the middle as a parenthetical clause. The actual point which was stated above is: on that Day, their ears, eyes, and other organs will testify against them, and the objective is to clarify: when a person's own organs will speak to open all his secrets, what other testimony or intercession can be of any use to him? The client's own testimony is heavier than the testimony of millions. Conjoining on this, He stated: 'and remember also: He created you the first time, and to Him you will be returned.' That is: if you have assumed that your return will be to those assumed deities whom you worship, this is a mere illusion. Those who have no share in creation and management, how can they become protectors and places of return!

The Meaning of 'Tastatiroon'

Some have interpreted 'tastatiroon' (you cover/hide yourselves) as 'takhafoon' (you fear). In their view, the meaning is: you did not fear that your ears, eyes, and other organs would testify against you. In my view, this explanation of the word is correct. This is an argument from the logical consequence, which can be found in every language. Obviously, if they had feared this, they would have tried to hide from them when committing an evil deed, but when they did not, it clearly means they did not fear any such thing from them.

'Rather, you assumed...'—meaning: let alone fearing the organs' testimony, your foolishness was such that you even assumed regarding God that He also is unaware of most of your deeds.

4. Subsequent Theme: Verses 25–36

First, the state of the leaders of disbelief and their followers on the Day of Judgment is pointed to—showing that today they have formed a coalition to oppose the Qur'an, but when its end appears before them, they will become enemies of one another, to the extent that the common people will pray to Allah to show them their leaders so that they can trample them under their feet.

After this, the state of those is described who remain firm on Tawhid despite all these oppositions. Allah's angels will descend upon them with glad tidings—stating: 'Your period of trial has ended; ahead for you is the Paradise of the eternal kingdom.'

Within this, the Prophet (pbuh) is consoled—stating that you have brought the most blessed invitation. If your foolish opponents do not value it, and instead of listening to it with attention, try to create disruption in it, you face their bad conduct with pardon and forgiveness. Your gracious attitude will prove to be a source of blessing for your invitation... Recite the verses.

5. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

The Consequence of Turning away from the Reminder of Allah

That is, the cause of their facing this end was that they turned away from the reminder of Allah, and instead of listening to the teaching of His messengers, disregarded it. As a retribution for this, Allah Almighty set bad leaders over them who showed them green pastures, until Allah's promise of punishment was fulfilled regarding them, as it was fulfilled regarding previous disobedient nations.

'And We appointed for them companions' points to that Sunnah of Allah which is mentioned in Surah Az-Zukhruf in these words: 'And whoever is blinded from remembrance of the Most Merciful - We appoint for him a devil, and he is to him a companion.' (36). These devils are from among the Jinn and also from among humans, and when a nation, due to its godlessness, sets such devils over itself, these devils do not leave them until they deliver them to the punishment of Allah.

'who made attractive to them what was before them and what was behind them'—meaning they portray the past and present of the nation to them in such a way that they see no deficiency in their deeds, character, and beliefs, so that they remain indifferent to the warnings of the prophets and continue on the path upon which they want them to walk.

'and the word has come into effect upon them' (wa haqqa 'alayhimu-l-qawl)—the word refers to the threat of Allah's punishment which He gave to those who denied the messengers, and which was fulfilled upon all nations who denied. The brief reference to the punishment of these nations has passed above in this Surah.

"And those who disbelieve say, 'Do not listen to this Qur'an and make noise therein that perhaps you will overcome.'" (26)

The Weapon of the Wicked to Block the Truth

'Those who disbelieve' (alladhina kafaroo) is general in words, but the context is proof that it refers specifically to those leaders of disbelief who were dominant over the Arab polytheists, and upon whose attitude a brief commentary was made in the preceding verse. It states: to turn people away from the invitation of the Qur'an, they instruct their gang: 'Whenever you see that Muhammad (pbuh) is reciting the Qur'an, neither listen to it yourselves nor let others listen, but raise such a clamor and hooting that his voice is completely suppressed and your voice remains higher.'

"But We will surely cause those who disbelieve to taste a severe punishment, and We will surely recompense them the worst of what they used to do." (27)

It states: let them do whatever they wish, We will also make them taste a severe punishment as a retribution for these actions of theirs, and We will bring before them the worst aspect of their deeds, of which they have no estimate today.

The Correct Estimate of the Effects of Deeds Occurs Only in the Hereafter

It should be kept in mind here that in this world, a person cannot form a correct estimate of the effects of their deeds. Every deed of man, whether good or bad, possesses infectious effects. Due to this characteristic, in some conditions, a small virtue of man grows to become like Mount Uhud. Similarly, an evil which appears ordinary at its initial stage gradually becomes a terrifying forest. Qabil (Cain) killed his brother Habil, initiating unjust murder, and this proved to be such an infectious evil that a share of every unjust murder in the world is continuously recorded in his account. This shows that when the results of everyone's deeds appear before them on the Day of Judgment, only then will they estimate what kind of evil their evil was. Pointing to this, He stated: 'and We will surely recompense them the worst of what they used to do'—meaning these simpletons do not estimate what a poisonous crop they are preparing for themselves by turning people away from the Qur'an and creating disruption in it; when its full harvest comes before them, only then will they see its worst aspect (aswa'a) and realize what a destruction they have prepared for themselves.

"That is the recompense of the enemies of Allah - the Fire. For them therein is the home of eternity as recompense for what they, Our signs, used to deny." (28)

'That' (dhalika) points to that worst recompense ('jaza'u aswa'a') which was mentioned in the preceding verse. 'The enemies of Allah' (a'da' Allah) refers, as is clear from the context, to those leaders of the Quraysh who tried to suppress the invitation of the Qur'an through their gang. 'The Fire' (An-Nar) is an independent sentence. To emphasize the predicate, the subject has been omitted. The meaning is: this recompense which has been described will be faced by those who are the enemies of Allah and His religion today, and understand well that this refers to the Fire. In it will be the homes of these leaders of disbelief, wherein they will abide eternally. This punishment will be meted out to them for the crime that they continuously denied the signs of Allah—neither paying attention to them themselves, nor letting others pay attention.

"And those who disbelieved will say, 'Our Lord, show us those who misled us of the jinn and men [so] we may put them under our feet that they will be among the lowest.'" (29)

The Anger of the Common People against Their Leaders

Here also, 'those who disbelieved' (alladhina kafaroo) is general in words, but the context is proof that it refers to those common people who were misled by their leaders—who neither paid attention to the Qur'an themselves nor let others do so. It states that the common people who are following their leaders with such devotion today, when they see where their leaders have led them, will grind their teeth in anger and pray: 'O our Lord, show us those Jinn and men who showed us this path, so that we may trample them under our feet to let them taste the retribution of their pride and leadership, and humiliate them to satisfy our hearts.'

"Indeed, those who have said, 'Our Lord is Allah' and then remained steadfast - the angels will descend upon them, [saying], 'Do not fear and do not grieve; and receive the glad tidings of Paradise, which you were promised.'" (30)

Glad Tidings for Those Who Remain Steadfast on Faith

After describing the fate of the opponents of truth on the Day of Judgment, this describes the state of those who accepted the invitation of the messenger and then remained firm on it, regardless of all opposition. It states: on the Day of Judgment, before the decision of affairs, to console and encourage them, Allah's angels will descend upon them, assuring them: 'Your period of trial has ended; now neither is there any fear for you, nor any grief; accept the glad tidings of that Paradise of which you were promised in the world through the prophets and messengers.'

"We were your allies in the worldly life and [are so] in the Hereafter. And you will have therein whatever your souls desire, and you will have therein whatever you request [i.e., demand]," (31)

The Believers Enjoy the Company of the Angels in this World as Well

That is, while the companions of the disbelievers were the devils from among Jinn and men who ultimately led them to Hell, your companions in the world were We, and in the Hereafter, We are your companions. Now, in the Paradise which you are to receive, you will have whatever your souls desire, and whatever you demand will be present for you. It is clear that the angels of Allah Almighty remain with the believers in this world as well. They guide them to the path of goodness and encourage them in the difficulties faced in this path. A person is deprived of their help only when he does not value their guidance and hands over his reins entirely to Satan. We have discussed the help and company of the angels in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Anfal.

"As accommodation from a [Lord Who is] Forgiving and Merciful." (32)

'Nuzul' refers to the initial hospitality presented to a guest. The meaning is: whatever you receive will be by way of initial hospitality. What is to be received after this is known only to Allah.

"And who is better in speech than one who invites to Allah and does righteousness and says, 'Indeed, I am of the Muslims.'" (33)

No Invitation is Better than the Invitation to Islam

This is said to console the Prophet (pbuh) and to reproach those opponents of yours who said: 'Our hearts are closed to your invitation, our ears are deaf, and between us and you is a partition' (5). The meaning is: if these people are not listening to your word, and in the madness of opposition have gone to the extent of instructing their gang not to listen to this Qur'an and to raise noise to suppress it, then there is no need to pursue them. It has become entirely clear that all the fault lies within these people themselves, and there is no fault in you or in your invitation. Your invitation is to the Tawhid of Allah and His worship. What invitation under this sky can be better than this! Along with this invitation, you yourself are the model of righteous deeds, and you are presenting this same invitation to others; this is an undeniable testimony to the fact that you are no artificial person, nor do you wish to cause any corruption in the land. Rather, your statement is: 'Indeed, I am of the Muslims' (I am of the obedient servants of my Lord)—which clearly means you are not striving to establish your own authority over the people, but wish to make everyone an obedient servant of Allah. This Islam is the nature of this entire universe, and this was the religion of Hazrat Ibrahim (peace be upon him) whose descendants these people are. If, despite these clear realities, they are compiled on opposing you, refer their matter to Allah. No one besides Allah can remove the covers from their hearts.

"And not equal are the good deed and the bad. Repel [evil] by that [deed] which is better; and thereupon the one whom between you and him is enmity [will become] as though he was a devoted friend." (34)

The Best Attitude for an Inviter toward the Opponents

This is the instruction of that attitude which you and your companions must adopt toward the bad conduct of the opponents. It states: the good deed and the bad deed are not equal. In terms of the end in the Hereafter and the objective of the invitation, there is a difference of heaven and earth in their results. For this reason, in response to their bad conduct, adopt that attitude which is better. Its benefit will be that those of your enemies who possess some integrity, being influenced by the correctness of your invitation and your noble attitude, will become your active supporters.

'Repel [evil] by that [deed] which is better' (idfa' billatee hiya ahsan) means that although in response to some bad conduct, adopting an equal attitude is permissible, yet the attitude of patience, pardon, and forgiveness is far better; therefore, Allah Almighty loves that His righteous servants adopt this attitude. This instruction has occurred in various places in the Qur'an in this very context. We present a parallel from Surah An-Nahl which explains this verse:

'Invite to the way of your Lord with wisdom and good instruction, and argue with them in a way that is best. Indeed, your Lord is most knowing of who has strayed from His way, and He is most knowing of who is [rightly] guided. And if you punish [them], punish with an equivalent of that with which you were harmed. But if you are patient - it is better for those who are patient.' (An-Nahl: 125-126).

This shows that although the right to avenge the offenses of the opponents is possessed, from the perspective of invitation and reform, the way of patience, pardon, and forgiveness is far better; therefore, the instruction was given to adopt this better way.

It should be kept in mind here that this attitude has been instructed to the believers only as long as proof is being established against the opponents. If proof was established but despite this they do not desist from opposition, but are rather bent upon wiping out the people of truth and the invitation of truth, then after an explicit declaration of disassociation, their complete suppression can also be made. In this case, instead of pardon and forgiveness, the better way—and indeed the only way in some conditions—will be this. Thus, those people of Makkah with whom the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions were instructed to adopt this attitude, with them, after the establishment of proof, they were instructed to adopt that attitude whose details have passed in Surah At-Tawbah.

A Noble Attitude Affects Those of Upright Nature Beautifully

'and thereupon the one whom between you and him is enmity [will become] as though he was a devoted friend'—this is said keeping in view the general nature of man. Those whose nature is not distorted, when they see that a person is so anxious for their welfare and guidance that despite all their insolence, he maintains his noble attitude and answers their stones with prayers, then even if they harbor enmity against him due to some misconception, being influenced by his conduct, their enmity changes into love, and they become his loyal companions. Thus, this is a fact: in the Prophet's (pbuh) invitation, his character held the position of the most effective factor. Those of your enemies who possessed any nobility were influenced by your character to become your loyal companions and the self-sacrificing ones of Islam. Only those wicked ones were not influenced whose nature was entirely distorted.

"And none is granted it except those who are patient, and none is granted it except one having a great portion [of good]." (35)

A Great Treasure

The referent of the object pronoun is not mentioned in words, but refers to that wisdom and advice which was instructed in the preceding verse. This method of bringing the pronoun is well-known in Arabic. Under verse 45 of Surah Al-Baqarah: 'and indeed, it is difficult except for the humbly submissive' (wa-innaha lakabeeratun), we have discussed the nature of this pronoun. It states: only those are granted this wisdom who possess the attribute of patience, and this wisdom is no ordinary thing, but a great treasure; highly fortunate are those who succeed in achieving it!

From this verse, three things are learned:

* First, what has been stated above is a great wisdom.

* Second, only those can hold this wisdom who possess the attribute of patience. Those who do not possess it are not worthy of it. For this reason, its seekers must establish the attribute of patience within themselves.

* Third, this is no ordinary thing, but an everlasting treasure; therefore, every ambitious person must play the game to achieve it. Highly fortunate are those who succeed in this game.

The Cure for a Whispering of Satan

"And if there comes to you from Satan an evil suggestion, then seek refuge in Allah. Indeed, He is the Hearing, the Knowing." (36)

This describes the cure—showing that if from the mischief of the opponents and the whispering of Satan, any such feeling arises in the heart which is contrary to this wisdom, at that time, a person should seek refuge in Allah Almighty. If, instead of being swept away by emotions, a person seeks the refuge of Allah with a sincere heart, Allah Almighty will protect him and will not let Satan gain control over him. Allah Almighty is the Hearing, the Knowing. No matter is hidden from Him. When a servant seeks His help against Satan with a sincere heart, He helps him.

6. Subsequent Theme: Verses 37–54

Next, the proofs of Tawhid and the Resurrection established from the signs of the horizons at the beginning of the Surah are explained further, along with warnings to those who are turning away from the guidance of Allah's signs and trying to turn others away. They are warned: the Qur'an is the revealed scripture of the Exalted in Might God. Those who deny it will harm neither God nor His messenger, but only prepare their own destruction. Within this, it is also pointed out: by sending down the Qur'an, Allah Almighty has bestowed a great favor upon the Arabs and the People of the Book. If they do not value this blessing, they will face its consequences. Allah has established proof against them; therefore, whatever end they face, they deserve it. This is no injustice to them... Recite the verses.

7. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Do Not Worship the Signs of God

'And of His signs are the night and the day and the sun and the moon. Do not prostrate to the sun or to the moon, but prostate to Allah, Who created them, if it should be Him you worship.' (37)

This verse relates to verses 9–12 above, which established proofs of Tawhid and the Resurrection from the creation of the heavens and the earth. The matters of warning, reminding, or consolation which passed in the middle are in accordance with the primary objective; because of this, no distance has been produced. Thus, conjoining on that, He stated: 'Among the signs of the Creator's power are this night and day, and the sun and moon.' They testify by their movement, their coming and going, and their rising and setting that they are not gods, nor are they partners of God in His divinity; but the Creator of the universe has joined them as parts of His machine, and they are performing their specified duties by His command. After this testimony of theirs, declaring them gods or partners of God is sheer ignorance. Their status is not of gods, but of the signs of His power, wisdom, mercy, and lordship, and there are countless other signs, among which these are.

'Do not prostrate to the sun or to the moon, but prostate to Allah, Who created them': This is the result of the preceding statement—showing that since they are His signs and Allah Almighty has subjected them to the service of man, worshipping them is an insult of your Lord and also of yourselves. The deserving recipient of worship is Allah alone, Who brought every single thing into existence and put them in the service of man. Prostration is used here in the sense of worship, because it is among the most prominent manifest forms of worship. The prohibition of the worship of the sun and moon is specifically made because the polytheistic nations of every era have worshipped these two.

'if it should be Him you worship'—meaning the right of Allah Almighty's pure worship is fulfilled only when no one else is prostrated to. Prostration is the greatest sign of worship; therefore, it is not permissible for anyone besides Allah Almighty. It should be kept in mind here that the polytheists' worship of others besides Allah was on the assumption that they are a means of proximity to God. As if their worship was, in their assumption, the worship of God. This clause refutes their assumption—showing that the worship of God must be so pure from any partnership that even in the specific signs of His worship, no one is associated.

"But if they are arrogant - then those who are near your Lord exalt Him by night and by day, and they do not become weary." (38)

God is Not in Need of Anyone's Worship

That is, if these people do not listen to your word out of arrogance, and say with great pomp: 'Our hearts are closed to your words, our ears are deaf, and between us and you is a partition,' then leave them to their state. God has no need of them, and you also need not care about them. If they do not worship God, His assembly is not going to become empty. The angels near your Lord are busy in His glorification day and night, and their passion is such that they never become weary of this work. 'Those who are near your Lord' (alladhina 'inda Rabbika) refers to the angels as a whole, not only to the close angels. This is because the attribute of the angels mentioned here is shared by all angels. 'near your Lord' is used to express that proximity to God which the angels enjoy compared to Jinn and humans. The meaning is: if these rebellious people are avoiding the worship of God, let them; those who are higher than them in status and whom they have made their deities do not disdain the worship of God, but are active in His praise and glorification day and night, and active in such a way that no difference occurs in their activity even for a single moment.

"And of His signs is that you see the earth barren, but when We send down upon it water, it quivers and grows. Indeed, He who has given it life is the Giver of life to the dead. Indeed, He is over all things competent." (39)

Reminding of the Resurrection

Following Tawhid, this is the reminding of the Resurrection—stating that those who consider the Resurrection improbable should observe the signs of Allah in this earth. You see that the earth lies entirely dry, lifeless, and without any greenery, until We send down Our rain of mercy upon it, and life appears within it before your eyes, its depression is filled with the growth of vegetation, and the beauty of growth appears in its every corner.

'Indeed, He who has given it life is the Giver of life to the dead'—it states that the God Who, by His power, continuously makes this earth die and live is competent to raise humans alive after their death. 'Indeed, He is over all things competent'—this is because Allah Almighty is competent over everything. If He were not competent over everything, He would not be God.

"Indeed, those who distort Our verses are not hidden from Us. So, is he who is cast into the Fire better or he who comes secure on the Day of Resurrection? Do what you will; indeed, He is Seeing of what you do." (40)

Warning the Wicked

The Meaning of 'Ilhad'

'Ilhad' means to deviate and choose a crooked way. 'those who distort Our verses' (yalhidoona fee ayatina) means: while the signs and verses of Allah are guiding in some other direction, a person chooses another path out of his defiance and stubbornness, and tries to turn others to it.

In verse 25, the efforts of bad companions and leaders have been mentioned. Such wicked ones are threatened in this verse—stating: those who are committing such actions are not hidden from Us; We are watching and hearing everything. The meaning is: since they are not hidden from Us, We will punish them one day. This is left implicit, and the wrath within this implication needs no explanation.

'So, is he who is cast into the Fire better or he who comes secure on the Day of Resurrection?'—this asks in an interrogative style. The meaning is: since Allah is watching everyone's actions, people should choose whatever way they choose after deciding this question: who has a good end—the one who is cast into the Fire or the one who will come secure of every fear on the Day of Judgment? Since His seeing and His power of recreating necessitate reward and punishment, whosoever has to do whatever in this world, let him do it after deciding whether he chooses Hell or Paradise. The paths of both are open before everyone. Whichever of these two paths a person considers better, let him choose it. Allah Almighty, after clarifying good and evil and their ends, has left the choice to everyone's discretion.

'Do what you will; indeed, He is Seeing of what you do'—this is the expression of that freedom of choice: Allah, after clarifying good and evil and their ends, has left you free to do good or evil; but remember that whatever you do, Allah is watching it. In this final clause, the warning is beyond description.

An Eloquent Omission

'Indeed, those who disbelieve in the message when it has come to them... And indeed, it is a mighty Book. Falsehood cannot approach it from before it or from behind it; [it is] a revelation from a [Lord Who is] Wise and Praiseworthy.' (41-42)

'The message' (Al-Dhikr) refers to the Holy Qur'an; the reasons for describing it as 'Dhikr' have passed. This resumes that very same theme which passed at the beginning of the Surah. It states: those who are denying the Qur'an when it has come to them... after this, its predicate (khabar) is omitted, and this omission is highly eloquent—showing that to express the severity of their crime, mentioning its gravity is sufficient. They will see that wretched Day which was faced by the deniers of previous messengers.

A Comprehensive Explanation

After 'disbelieve in the message' (kafaroo bidh-dhikr), the words 'when it has come to them' (lamma ja'ahum) are also worthy of attention. This is to express the extreme gravity of their crime. If a person has not seen a thing, and has not got an opportunity to evaluate its beauty, and he disregards it, he may be considered excused to some extent; but the one who has seen the Qur'an, evaluated its miraculous nature, and tested the strength of its proofs, if he denies it, he is denying the sun when it is shining over his head.

Referring to Some Attributes of the Qur'an

In 'And indeed, it is a mighty Book' (Wa-innahu la-kitabun 'aziz), there are two aspects: first of warning and threat, and second of the purity and sanctity of the Qur'an. In terms of warning, it relates to the preceding theme, and in terms of the second, it is the introduction to the subsequent verse.

'Aziz' means dominant and powerful. In this sense, it presents proof for the threat—showing that those who are denying the Qur'an should remember that this Qur'an is not the request of a petitioner, but its sender is 'Al-Aziz'—meaning the Dominant and Powerful, and this Book is itself 'Aziz'; therefore, its status is of a command which must be obeyed. Those who deny it should remember that this Book will decide their affairs.

'Aziz' also means 'manee' (unreachable). 'Manee' is that which is beyond anyone's reach. Its explanation is coming in the subsequent verse. Here, just remember: no matter how much the devils of Jinn and men try, they cannot create any disruption in it. Falsehood can enter it neither from before it nor from behind it. Allah Almighty has protected it from the intrusion of the devils from every side. This is a reply to that failed attempt of theirs which was mentioned above: 'And those who disbelieve say, "Do not listen to this Qur'an and make noise therein that perhaps you will overcome."' (26).

'Falsehood cannot approach it from before it or from behind it; [it is] a revelation from a [Lord Who is] Wise and Praiseworthy'—this is a comprehensive description of the Qur'an's greatness, of which two aspects are highly important:

This Entire House is Illuminated by the Sun

First, the Qur'an is entirely protected from before it and from behind it. Allah Almighty has sent it down, Hazrat Jibreel brought it, Hazrat Muhammad (pbuh) held it, and the best of the creation accepted it. As if, from the beginning to the end, this entire house is illuminated by the sun, with no opening for the devils to intrude, neither from its beginning nor from its end.

Second, Allah Almighty Himself took the responsibility of protecting the Qur'an, as is clear from: 'and indeed, We are its guardian' (Al-Hijr: 9), and this is that distinction of the Qur'an which previous scriptures did not achieve. Protecting previous scriptures was entrusted to their holders, who could not fulfill their right, which resulted in their being distorted; but Allah Himself took the responsibility of protecting the Qur'an, and kept it safe from every mixture until the Resurrection.

There are several aspects of this protection:

Some Aspects of the Qur'an's Protection

* During the period of the Qur'an's sending down, Allah Almighty made special arrangements so that the devils could make no intervention in its revelation. Although in the system of the universe there is a permanent arrangement to prevent the devils from hearing the exalted assembly, we will clarify in the commentary of Surah Al-Jinn that during the sending down of the Qur'an, this arrangement was specifically made to prevent any intervention, so that they had no opportunity to introduce any falsehood into it from before it (min bayni yadayh).

* Second, the angel chosen for this task is described in the Qur'an as: 'possessing power, obeyed, strong, trustworthy, and secure in position with the Lord of the Throne.' That is, he is so powerful that the evil spirits cannot dominate him, he is the chief of all angels, and he cannot forget anything. The trust given to him from Allah, he delivers it precisely. It is impossible for any difference to occur in it. He is highly close to Allah, which is proof that in terms of capabilities, he is superior to all creations... Obviously, this arrangement was also made to prevent any falsehood from entering it from its source.

* Third, the human chosen to bear this trust was himself the best of creation, and the responsibility of remembering and protecting it was not placed upon him alone, but Allah took this responsibility upon Himself. Thus, in Surah Al-Qiyamah, He stated: 'Move not your tongue with it to hasten with it. Indeed, upon Us is its collection and its recitation. So when We have recited it, then follow its recitation. Then indeed, upon Us is its clarification.' (16–19). Traditions prove that whatever part of the Qur'an was revealed, the Prophet (pbuh) and his companions remembered it, and during every Ramadan, the Prophet (pbuh) revised it with Hazrat Jibreel to avoid any lapse, and this revision was in accordance with the sequence in which Allah Almighty willed to structure it. It is also known from traditions that in the final Ramadan of his life, the Prophet (pbuh) revised it twice. Then, in accordance with this sequence and recitation, the entire Qur'an was recorded in writing, and later, the Righteous Caliphs sent copies of it to other cities of the kingdom. No other previous scripture received such care. To the extent that regarding the Torah, no one knows when and by whose hands its various books were structured.

* Fourth, the Qur'an is a miracle in terms of its eloquence of words and depth of meaning. Because of this, no other discourse can be mixed with it. Even the Prophet's (pbuh) own discourse, despite his being its presenter and the most eloquent of all, cannot match it; for this reason, there is no possibility of any other discourse being mixed with it. Thus, the specimens of those claimants who tried to present a parallel of the Qur'an exist in the books of literature and history. Compare them with the Qur'an, and you will find a vast difference... in this way, the way for any intrusion from behind (min khalfihi) was blocked.

* Fifth, along with protecting the Qur'an, Allah Almighty promised to protect its language until the Resurrection. In other heavenly scriptures, due to their languages being lost, countless distortions entered through translations; but the original language of the Qur'an is protected and will remain protected until the Resurrection; for this reason, there is no possibility of any falsehood entering it through translations and commentaries, and if anyone attempts to do so, those of knowledge can identify and separate it.

We have pointed to some prominent aspects here. There are other aspects as well, but this is an independent subject. From these few points, you can estimate why the Qur'an has this status: falsehood cannot approach it from before it or from behind it.

'a revelation from a [Lord Who is] Wise and Praiseworthy'—the word 'Tanzeel' (revelation) contains the meaning of care and graduation; therefore, its meaning is: this Book has been sent down with highly elaborate care from the Wise and Praiseworthy God. Two attributes—'Hakeem' (Wise) and 'Hameed' (Praiseworthy)—are referred to here. 'Hakeem' means His every word and action is based on wisdom; therefore, He has sent down this wise Book with great care. 'Hameed' means the deserving recipient of praise, Who is the source of all praise; therefore, He has blessed His creation with this great blessing purely out of His favor. In the preceding verse, 'Al-Aziz' (Exalted in Might) was referred to, which contained the aspect of threat. In this verse, 'Hakeem' and 'Hameed' contain the aspect of encouragement.

"Nothing is said to you, [O Muhammad], except what was already said to the messengers before you. Indeed, your Lord is a possessor of forgiveness and a possessor of painful penalty." (43)

Consolation for the Prophet (pbuh)

This is said to console the Prophet (pbuh)—stating: do not assume that your nation alone has adopted this attitude toward you; rather, what is being said to you is what the wicked of other nations had already said to their respective messengers. This is a shared tradition between the messengers and their nations. The meaning is: it is not due to any deficiency on your part that the wicked of the nation have become your enemies, but this has always happened with the messengers; so just as other resolute messengers bore their nations' offenses with patience, you also observe patience.

'Indeed, your Lord is a possessor of forgiveness and a possessor of painful penalty'—meaning rest assured that there is delay with God, but not injustice. If He is granting respite to these people despite all their offenses, it does not mean He has no law of reward and punishment. He is highly Forgiving, and also Severe in punishment. He grants respite until the final limit so that those who wish to deserve His forgiveness may do so; but when they do not benefit from this respite or misuse it, He punishes them so severely that no one else can punish like Him.

"And if We had made it a foreign Qur'an, they would have said, 'Why are its verses not explained in detail [in our language]? Is it a foreign [tongue] and an Arab [messenger]?' Say, 'It is, for those who believe, a guidance and cure.' And those who do believe not - in their ears is deafness, and it is upon them blindness. Those are being called from a distant place." (44)

An Objection Inspired by the Jews, and Its Reply

Some of those excuses are mentioned which the opponents devised to turn away from the Qur'an, and their absurdity is clarified. The context indicates that some of these other objections were originally inspired by the Jews, who devised them to deprive the Children of Ismail of the blessing of the Qur'an; but the foolish leaders of the Quraysh, being unaware of their malice and plots, in the heat of opposing the Prophet (pbuh), began to repeat the objections inspired by them. Several objections taught by the Jews which the leaders of the Quraysh adopted have passed in the previous Surahs, and will also come in the subsequent Surahs. Among them was the objection: 'The specific language of revelation until now has been Hebrew, in which all those scriptures were revealed whose heavenly status is accepted even by the Qur'an. So why did God change His language, and why has this new revelation been sent down in Arabic!'

The Qur'an replied: this objection of theirs is merely for the sake of objection and an excuse to oppose the Qur'an. If the Qur'an had been sent down in some foreign language, these very people would have raised the objection: why have its verses not been explained in detail in our own language? But when We sent it down in the Arabic language to explain it clearly to them, instead of being grateful to Allah Almighty for this favor, they stood up with this objection inspired by the enemies: why did Allah Almighty send down this revelation in Arabic, contrary to the previous tradition! As if a great favor of Allah Almighty became a cause of objection for them!

'Is it a foreign [tongue] and an Arab [messenger]?'—this is a part of their objection: at that time, they would make the excuse: 'The message is foreign, and the addressee is Arab!'—implying: what absurdity is this, that those who are the primary addressees of this Book are entirely unfamiliar with its language!

'The language of my beloved is Turkish, and I do not know Turkish.'

'Say, "It is, for those who believe, a guidance and cure."' It states: tell these people that this blessing is for those who value it. Those who do not want to believe will find some backdoor to oppose it, but those who seek guidance and who feel their intellectual and spiritual diseases will find within it both guidance and the cure for their pain.

'And those who do believe not - in their ears is deafness, and it is upon them blindness'—meaning the cause of their not believing is not that there is some deficiency in this Book, because of which it is not appealing to their hearts, but their own ears are deaf. The capability to accept the truth is dead within them; because of this, its voice does not affect their hearts, but returns after hitting their ears.

'and it is upon them blindness'—similarly, those who are not guided by it are not guided because this is not the light of guidance, but because this light has dazed their vision, because of which it has become a cause of blindness for them instead of guidance.

The meaning is: these people had made their eyes used to darkness instead of light; because of this, when this heavenly light appeared, their eyes could not bear its intensity, and even their remaining light was stripped away. To fully understand this, have a look at what we wrote under verse 20 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Those Who Do Not Hear from Nearby Will Be Forced to Hear from Afar

'Those are being called from a distant place'—meaning today they are being called from very nearby: the messenger of Allah is following them with His Book and His message, but they, out of their arrogance, do not listen to any word, as if they hear nothing. But they should remember that soon that Day is coming when the caller of the Resurrection will call them from a distant place, and they will run toward his call immediately. They will not be able to show any delay or deviation.

In Surah Ta-Ha, this theme is expressed as follows:

'That Day, they will follow the Caller; no deviance [is allowed] to him. And voices will be humbled before the Most Merciful, so you will hear not but a whisper.' (108).

To understand this verse, have a look at verses 1-5 of this Surah, particularly verse 5: 'And they say, "Our hearts are closed to that to which you invite us, and in our ears is deafness, and between us and you is a partition."' The arrogant said this by way of pride—claiming that their hearts were closed, their ears deaf, and a partition was set up; but the Qur'an confirmed their every word, and also clarified the nature of their deafness, blindness, and partition, and on what Day and how these diseases of theirs will be cured.

Another Objection Inspired by the Jews, and Its Reply

This verse has passed in verse 110 of Surah Hud, where we have commented on it. This is, in reality, a reply to an objection of the same nature as that which was replied to in the preceding verse. The Jews taught this objection to the people: 'When the Torah, the Book of Allah, is present and its status as a divine Book is accepted even by the Qur'an, why was there any need to send down a new Book?' The Qur'an replied to this objection from various aspects in different Surahs. Here, the reply given is: 'It is a fact that We gave Moses the Book, but along with this, it is also an undeniable reality that differences and contradictions were created in it.' 'and difference was created in it' (fa-khtulifa feehi) refers here to that contradiction which is found in various books of the Torah. If a difference is merely of interpretation, it can be resolved by referring to the source and other parallels; but when there is open contradiction in the source itself, then resolving this difference is impossible. Thus, it is a fact that in different parts of the Torah, a single matter has been described in such conflicting ways that reaching the actual reality is highly difficult. Several examples of the contradictions of the Torah have passed in our book. Here, we point only to that conflict of statements which is found in the Torah regarding the prophecies of the advent of the unlettered Prophet (pbuh).
