If the Jews had not created these contradictions through distortion, they would have faced no difficulty in understanding why Allah Almighty sent down the Qur'an after the Torah, and why Hazrat Muhammad (pbuh) was sent as a messenger after Hazrat Musa (peace be upon him) to fulfill that deficiency of religion. But the Jews, due to their misfortune, tried to conceal the reality, and in this way, prepared not only their own deviation, but also carried the responsibility of the deviation of a vast creation.

The reason for the occurrence of such contradictions in the Torah has been pointed to—showing that it did not receive that protection which the Qur'an received. It faced such calamities several times that the entire Torah was lost. Those who structured it later, structured it merely from their memory, and it is also not known who they were. Some of its books were kept secret, because they contained matters whose general circulation was considered contrary to their interests by the Jewish priests. In such matters, they made distortions according to their own desires, and succeeded in this. The occurrence of contradictions in such a book is not in the least surprising, and its logical consequence is that the actual reality is completely lost, people are engulfed again in the darkness from which Allah Almighty wanted to deliver them by sending down this light, and such differences are created among them which cannot be resolved.

A Severe Threat

'And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord, it would have been judged between them'—this is a highly severe threat, and since this objection was inspired by the Jews, the threat is also directed at them. The meaning is: the Jews still consider themselves the holders of the Torah and are in the delusion that because of the Torah, the leadership they enjoyed remains with them and cannot be challenged. Whereas, after the injustice they have committed against the Book of Allah, they deserved that a decision be made between them through God's court and His punishment; but Allah Almighty has appointed a time for the decision of every nation; because of this, they have been granted respite. However, this respite is after all respite, which will end one day, and they will face the consequences of their injustice.

The Consequence of Differences in the Torah

'and indeed, they are concerning it in disquieting doubt' (wa-innahum la-fee shakkin minhu mureeb)—this describes the consequence of that difference and contradiction which was created in the Torah. Obviously, its consequence was that every single thing of the Torah became doubtful in the eyes of the holders of the Torah themselves, due to which distinguishing between truth and falsehood became impossible. Many factions were created among them, and every faction made whatever suited its desires its religion, and the actual religion was lost in these contradictions.

According to Imam Razi, the referent of the pronoun in 'minhu' (concerning it) is the Qur'an, but in my view, its referent is the Torah, and the aspect of its being doubtful is what has passed above.

This theme is also coming in Surah Ash-Shura, where some اجمالات of this verse will be clarified. In Ash-Shura, it is expressed as follows:

'And they did not become divided until after knowledge had come to them - out of jealous animosity between themselves. And if not for a word that preceded from your Lord [postponing punishment] until a specified term, it would have been concluded between them. And indeed, those who were granted inheritance of the Scripture after them are, concerning it, in disquieting doubt.' (Ash-Shura: 14).

"Whoever does righteousness - it is for [the benefit of] his soul; and whoever does evil - it is against it. And your Lord is not ever unjust to [His] servants." (46)

Declaration of Self-Sufficiency from the Opponents

This is an expression of self-sufficiency from them. It states: neither is any benefit to be achieved by God from anyone's virtue, nor any harm from anyone's evil. Whoever does righteousness, its benefit will be achieved by them alone; and whoever does evil, its consequence will fall upon them alone. Allah Almighty is not in the least unjust to His servants. He will place the results and consequences of everyone's deeds before them. The meaning is: this activity of Allah and His messenger to show the straight path to the people should not lead anyone to the misconception that any work of God is stuck without people's guidance; rather, all this effort is for the benefit of the people. Allah Almighty does not desire that people are caught in His grasp while proof has not been fully established against them. In verse 15 of Surah Al-Jathiyah, this theme has passed in this very context.

The rule of this style in Arabic has been explained—showing that when negation is placed upon exaggeration (zallam), it is meant to emphasize the negation; therefore, 'And your Lord is not ever unjust to [His] servants' (Wa ma Rabbuka bi-zallamin lil-'abeed) means: 'And your Lord is not in the least unjust to His servants.'

8. Subsequent Theme: Verses 47–54

Next are the concluding verses of the Surah, in which first those who were carefree regarding the punishment or the Resurrection because they were not told its exact time are warned. They are informed: denying any reality merely because its time of occurrence is unknown is no wisdom. There are so many obvious realities whose time of occurrence is unknown, yet no wise person denies them.

After this, regret is expressed over the state of those who are hastening for the punishment—showing that the respite Allah has granted them out of His favor for their repentance and reform is considered by them as mere boasting, and they are demanding the punishment; whereas if they are caught even slightly in His grasp, they will not tire of making long supplications to escape from it.

Finally, they are warned of the terrifying end of denying the Qur'an, and informed: if the proofs of its truthfulness described so far are not sufficient for them, soon those signs of horizons and ourselves will appear, whose denial no one will dare to make... Recite the verses.

9. Analysis of Words and Explanation of Verses

Ayah 47

Replying to Those Who Mock the Resurrection

This is the reply given to those who, by way of mockery, asked: 'When will it be?' (Mata huwa?), 'When will it appear?', 'If it has to come, why does it not come?', 'When will this ship sail and land on the shore?' To such mockers, the Qur'an has replied in various places that the coming of the Resurrection is a reality. This is a self-evident requirement of this universe and the attributes of its Creator. The horizons, ourselves, reason, and nature are its witnesses. Therefore, it is bound to come, but as for the question: when will it come? Its knowledge is with Allah alone. No one besides Him knows this secret.

'And no fruits emerge from their sheaths nor does any female conceive or give birth except with His knowledge'—and this is not the only secret whose knowledge is with God alone; rather, there are so many other obvious realities whose knowledge is with Allah alone, and man's knowledge regarding them is highly limited, but despite this, no wise person denies their being real. A wheat ear appears; no one knows whether it will ripen, and if it does, how many grains will be bad, how many good, how many will go to waste, how many will be protected, what is the exact date of its ripening, how many grains are in the farmer's destiny to reach his store, and how many will go to the beasts and birds. The knowledge of all these matters is with Allah Almighty alone; therefore, if the exact time of the Resurrection's occurrence is not clear to man, there is no wonder in it. The Resurrection is a highly great thing; man's state is such that he has very little knowledge of even the ordinary things of his daily life.

'nor does any female conceive or give birth except with His knowledge'—a female conceives; who knows whether she will give birth to a girl or a boy, normal or abnormal, dead or alive, and when she will give birth? The correct knowledge of none of these matters is with anyone besides Allah Almighty. If man's knowledge is so limited regarding such a common thing, then if he cannot know or has not been told the exact time of the Resurrection's occurrence, what is there to wonder? Yes, if he could claim that he has achieved the knowledge of all other things, and only this one thing has remained outside his knowledge, then indeed it could be permissible for him to deny it on this basis. This same theme is expressed in another place as follows:

'Indeed, Allah [alone] has knowledge of the Hour and sends down the rain and knows what is in the wombs. And no soul perceives what it will earn tomorrow, and no soul perceives in what land it will die. Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Acquainted.' (Luqman: 34).

The Polytheists Will Deny Their Shirk on the Day of Judgment

'And the Day He will call to them, "Where are My partners?" they will say, "We announce to You that there is not among us any witness [to that]."'—meaning if this indifference toward the Resurrection is because they rely on their assumed partners, they must remember that on that Day, when Allah Almighty commands them to call those whom they assumed as His partners to save them from His grasp, they will reply: 'We announce to You that none of us is now ready to testify that there is any partner of Yours.' This has passed in the preceding Surah—Surah Ghafir.

'Then it will be said to them, "Where are those you used to associate besides Allah?" They will say, "They have departed from us; rather, we did not used to invoke prior [to this] anything." Thus does Allah leave astray the disbelievers.' (Ghafir: 73-74).

The meaning is: the intoxication of devotion to their assumed partners will vanish at the very first call.

Some have interpreted 'We announce to You that there is not among us any witness [to that]' (Adhannaka ma minna min shaheed) as the statement of their assumed partners—meaning when Allah Almighty demands of the polytheists: 'Where are those partners whom you assumed?', the angels, prophets, or righteous ones whom they worshipped will step forward to clarify their position, stating that none of them is ready to testify that there is any partner of Yours. This interpretation has probably been adopted in the light of the following verse:

'And the Day He will gather them and that which they worship besides Allah and will say, "Did you mislead these, My servants, or did they [themselves] stray from the path?" They will say, "Exalted are You! It was not for us to take any allies besides You. But You provided comforts for them and their fathers until they forgot the message and became a ruined people."' (Al-Furqan: 17-18).

In case of adopting the first interpretation, 'adhannaka' (we announce to You) will be in the sense of expressing disassociation. The meaning is: 'Now placing our hands on our ears, we admit our crime and hope that You show mercy to us.'

In case of adopting the second interpretation, the meaning will be: in reply to Allah Almighty, they will say what is mentioned in the aforementioned verse of Surah Al-Furqan; but when those who claimed their worship are demanded: 'Call your assumed partners,' they will refer to that statement which they had already made, and which is mentioned in the verse of Al-Furqan.

"And lost from them will be those they were invoking before, and they will be certain that they have no place of escape." (48)

Whether the worshippers express their disassociation from their deities or the deities from their worshippers, in both cases, the polytheists' ruin is certain. This is because those upon whose reliance they ignored the Hereafter will all vanish, and they will become certain that there is no place of escape for them from God's punishment.

The word 'zannu' (they will be certain) has been discussed in its proper place—stating that in such places, it is used in the sense of certainty (yaqeen) where the objective is to describe some nadeedah reality of the future which is, in the speaker's view, certain. For example: 'Indeed, I was certain that I would meet my account' (Al-Haqqah: 20).

"Man does not weary of supplication for good [things], but if evil touches him, he is hopeless and despairing." (49)

A Strange Weakness of Man

The word 'man' (Al-Insan) is general, but here it points specifically to those deniers who were hastening for the Resurrection—claiming that if it has to come, why does it not come! Instead of addressing them directly or referring to them, the statement is made in a general form so that aversion to them is expressed, and the statement also takes the form of a general rule.

It states: this man is a strange creation. If he receives favor and comfort, instead of being grateful to Us, he challenges Us and demands the punishment; and if he is caught even slightly in Our grasp, he does not weary of making long supplications to escape from it, and covenants that if he is saved, he will always remain Our grateful and obedient servant; but this is mere deception on his part. When We save him from this adversity, he returns to the same self-forgetfulness in which he was before; and if We do not save him or the adversity is prolonged, instead of being pleased with and patient over God's decision, he becomes entirely hopeless and despairing.

Generally, people have interpreted this verse as: such people are highly active in praying for their worldly successes. In their view, 'supplication for good' refers to the prayer for worldly progress; but this is entirely contrary to general experience. Such people, as long as their ship is sailing smoothly, never turn to God; but remain lost in their own ambitions and desires. However, when the ship is caught in some whirlpool, only then do they remember God, and at that time, they make long supplications themselves and also request others to do so. If the ship escapes from the whirlpool, as soon as it escapes, they forget God again, and consider this the miracle of their own planning or their own high-fortune; and if the adversity is prolonged, they completely despair.

This theme is expressed in various places in the Qur'an. For example, in Surah Luqman:

'And when waves come over them like canopies, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion. But when He delivers them to the land, some of them are moderate [in their beliefs]. And none rejects Our signs except every treacherous ingrate.' (Luqman: 32).

In Surah Yunus, this same theme is expressed as follows:

'And when affliction touches man, he calls upon Us, [lying] on his side or sitting or standing; but when We remove from him his affliction, he continues [on his way] as if he had never called upon Us to [remove] an affliction that touched him.' (Yunus: 12).

This same theme is also expressed in Surah Yunus in these words:

'He it is who enables you to travel on land and sea until, when you are in the ships and they sail with them and they rejoice therein, there comes a storm wind and the waves come at them from every side and they suppose that they are engulfed, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion, "If You should save us from this, we will surely be among the thankful." But when He saves them, at once they commit injustice upon the earth without right.' (Yunus: 22-23).

In the light of these parallels, in my view, the meaning of the verse under discussion is: such humans, when they fall into some trial, do not weary of making supplications for their good; thus below in verse 51, its explanation is also given: 'but when evil touches him, then he is full of extensive supplication.'

'but if evil touches him, he is hopeless and despairing': In the first stage of adversity, he makes long supplications, but when he sees that the supplications are not bearing fruit, despairing of God's mercy, he becomes entirely hopeless and despairing.

The meaning is: this category of people forget God in favor, remember Him only in adversity, and even in adversity, the nature of their relation with God is: when the adversity is removed, they forget Him again, and if it does not appear to be removed, they despair. Both of these are forms of disbelief and ungratefulness. The correct believer's conduct is: a person remains grateful to his Lord in favor and comfort, and when some trial is faced, faces it with patience and remains hopeful of his Lord's mercy. In other words, he does not boast in favor, and does not despair and complain in adversity. This is the true style of faith and trust, and such servants achieve the station of 'the soul at peace' and 'well-pleased and pleasing [to Him]'.

"And if We let him taste mercy from Us after adversity has touched him, he will surely say, 'This is [due] to me, and I do not think the Hour will occur; and if I should be returned to my Lord, indeed, there will be for me with Him the best [reward].' But We will surely inform those who disbelieved of what they did, and We will surely make them taste a massive punishment." (50)

Another Weakness

That is, if the adversity from which he seeks release is not removed, then, as in the preceding verse, he despairs of God; and if He removes his adversity to bless him with His favor, instead of being grateful to Him, he falls into the pride: 'This is my right. I was born with this honor and dignity, and I will live in it; no one can deprive me of it, nor do I need to be grateful to anyone for it. As for the Resurrection of which some people are warning me, first, I do not assume it will occur; and even if it is to occur and I must return to my Lord, there too my status and rank will be maintained and my end will be highly magnificent, because my world is magnificent.'

It should be kept in mind here that it is not necessary that such people say these words with their tongue (though many do say so with audacity); rather, the presence of these thoughts in the mind is sufficient. In the Qur'an, a person's mindset is expressed as a statement, because a person's conduct is the most accurate reflection of his inner state.

A Warning to the Dreamers of Sweet Dreams

'But We will surely inform those who disbelieved...'—this is a highly severe warning to those who are living in a fool's paradise. It states: these foolish people are dreaming that just as they enjoy comfort in the world, they will enjoy everything in the Hereafter (if it is to occur); so they should remember that the Hereafter is inevitable, and in it We will inform these disbelievers of their deeds. 'We will inform them of their deeds' is meant to point to its necessary consequence, which was clarified with 'and We will surely make them taste a massive punishment' (walonudheeqannahum min 'adhabin ghaleedh)—meaning We will make them taste a severe punishment.

"And when We bestow favor upon man, he turns away and distances himself; but when evil touches him, then he is full of extensive supplication." (51)

By 'distances himself' (na'a bi-janibihi), the same theme is expressed as has passed with 'turned away on his side' (tawalla bi-ruknihi) or 'bent his side' (thaniya 'itfihi) in other places. This is a depiction of turning away from the truth out of pride and arrogance. 'Janib' means side; when a person turns away from someone with pride, he shrugs his shoulders, turns his side, and departs. This state is expressed here with 'na'a bi-janibihi'.

A Specific Aspect of the Narrow-Minded Character

In the preceding verses, one aspect of such people's character was presented. Now, another aspect of their character is shown—stating: the condition of these narrow-minded people (pointing to the pharaohs of the Quraysh and the people of Ta'if) is: when We bestow Our favor upon them, they turn away from Us and boast with pride; and if they are caught even slightly in Our grasp, they become full of extensive supplications. That is: this pomp of theirs has no foundation. They are easily carried away in favor, and easily cry out in adversity.

"Say, 'Have you considered: if the Qur'an is from Allah and you disbelieved in it, who is more astray than one who is in extreme dissension?'" (52)

A Question to the Deniers of the Qur'an

This is a question to those deniers by way of concession—asking: if you are denying the Qur'an with this pomp, what if it is indeed from Allah Almighty! In this case, who will be more astray than those who are caught in such extreme dissension as to reach that stage of destruction from where no return is possible!

An Invitation to Reflect on the Qur'an with Seriousness

This is an invitation to reflect on the Qur'an with seriousness and in view of its actual importance, before making any decision regarding it. The foundation of this invitation is: the end of which the Qur'an is warning its deniers, and the proofs with which it is warning, are not things to be ignored easily or mocked; but rather it is the news of a highly terrifying end, supported by highly powerful proofs; therefore, those who are denying it with such audacity should at least not ignore the possibility of its claim being true. If they feel the possibility of its truth (and no stubborn person can deny its possibility), then wisdom requires and foresight demands that they reflect on this Qur'an, and whatever decision they make, they make after reflecting on its consequences. If they accept it, they will lose nothing, but will find everything; and if out of stubbornness and hostility they deny it, this hostility will carry them to a distant place from where no return will be possible... the meaning is: reflect on this issue in view of its actual importance. If pride, arrogance, envy, and hostility are allowed to interfere, this conflict will end with a highly destructive end.

"We will show them Our signs in the horizons and within themselves until it becomes clear to them that it is the truth. But is it not sufficient concerning your Lord that He is, over all things, a Witness?" (53)

The Proofs of the Qur'an's Truthfulness in the Horizons and Ourselves

This consoled the Prophet (pbuh) and threatened the deniers of the Qur'an. The meaning is: if these people are not ready to believe in the Qur'an on the basis of its proofs, and are rather bent upon seeing Our signs for its confirmation, then soon that time is coming when in the surroundings of Makkah and in Makkah itself, among the Quraysh, such signs of its truthfulness will stand manifest that these people will cry out: 'Indeed, the Qur'an is entirely the truth.'

By 'signs' (ayat), those proofs and signs of the dominance of the truth and the defeat of falsehood are meant of which the Qur'an has prophesied. This prophecy has passed in this Surah in the light of historical proofs. Initially, the leaders of the Quraysh attributed these to empty boasting and mocked them; but when in the surroundings of Makkah and in Makkah itself, among the good people of the Quraysh, Islam began to take root, they and their supporters were somewhat warned. Ultimately, after migration, such events of the dominance of Islam occurred that let alone the Quraysh, it became impossible even for Rome and Persia to stand against Islam. This theme is also in verse 93 of Surah An-Naml: 'And He will show you His signs, and you will recognize them.'

'But is it not sufficient concerning your Lord that He is, over all things, a Witness?'—meaning this glad tidings of the dominance of the truth being given to you is indeed regarding the future, but your Lord is aware of everything of the past, present, and future; so rest assured that His every word will be fulfilled.

"Unquestionably, they are in doubt about the meeting with their Lord. Unquestionably, He is, of all things, encompassing." (54)

At the end, the primary disease of these opponents is exposed—showing that their actual disease is that they are in doubt regarding the meeting with their Lord. This doubt of theirs has made them short-sighted in the affairs of life and bold in opposing the truth. Inform them clearly that Allah Almighty encompasses everything, and nothing is outside His sphere of power. He will do whatever He wills, and whenever He wills. No one can escape from His grasp, nor can anyone obstruct His will.

By the grace of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed on these lines. Praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds.

Rahman Abad
24 October, 1975 AD
18 Shawwal, 1395 AH.
